agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to a core logistics domain concept — the delivery promise — which directly shapes customer expectations, operational accountability, and SLA measurement.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery time communicated to the recipient. Represents the delivery promise."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is expected to occur. Can be expressed as an exact timestamp, a time range, or a date-only promise."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment made to the customer about when their package will arrive. Drives customer expectations and is the basis for SLA compliance measurement."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual commitment defining delivery performance standards, often measured against the communicated ETA."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient. Most variable and expensive segment, where ETA precision matters most."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"KPI measuring the percentage of deliveries completed within the communicated delivery promise. Directly affected by how the promise is defined."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery promise must be communicated to the customer at the time of order confirmation and remain visible throughout the delivery lifecycle.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without a communicated delivery promise, the customer has no expectation to measure against, and the business has no accountability metric. This is a fundamental logistics invariant.","testable_assertion":"Every order in 'confirmed' or later status has an associated delivery promise visible to the customer."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Changing the granularity of the delivery promise (timestamp vs date-only) redefines what constitutes an 'on-time' delivery. A date-only promise means any delivery on that calendar date is on-time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The definition of on-time delivery is coupled to the precision of the promise. If the promise is 'Friday', then 23:59 on Friday is on-time. This is a logical invariant — you cannot measure compliance against a promise that doesn't exist.","testable_assertion":"If ETA is date-only, a delivery completed at any time on the promised date is classified as on-time."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Existing in-flight deliveries with timestamp-based ETAs must not retroactively have their promises downgraded to date-only without customer notification.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Changing a delivery promise after it was made violates customer trust and potentially contractual obligations. State integrity of the original promise must be preserved.","testable_assertion":"Orders created before the cutover retain their original timestamp-based ETA format until delivered."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"B2B/enterprise customers with SLA contracts specifying time-based delivery windows may require continued timestamp-level precision regardless of the default display change.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy that can be negotiated per contract. A VP could decide to grandfather certain contracts or renegotiate terms.","testable_assertion":"Enterprise customers with time-based SLA contracts continue to receive timestamp-precision ETAs after the change."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The on-time delivery rate KPI must be recalibrated against the new promise granularity to avoid artificially inflating performance metrics.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational reporting policy. The measurement approach can be adjusted, but the business must consciously decide how to handle the metric discontinuity.","testable_assertion":"Historical on-time delivery reports include a notation when the promise granularity changed, and dashboards do not blend timestamp-based and date-based metrics without disclosure."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer places a new order after the cutover date","when":"The system calculates and displays the delivery ETA","then":"The ETA is shown as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no sub-day timestamp"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order was placed before the cutover with a timestamp-based ETA","when":"The customer views their order status after the cutover","then":"The original timestamp-based ETA is preserved and displayed as originally promised"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery occurs at 23:50 on the promised date","when":"The system evaluates on-time delivery compliance","then":"The delivery is classified as on-time since it was completed within the promised date"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An enterprise customer has a contract specifying 2-hour delivery windows","when":"The system calculates their delivery ETA after cutover","then":"The system continues to show timestamp-precision ETAs per their contractual SLA"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A customer receives a date-only ETA of 'Delivery by Friday'","when":"Friday arrives and the package has not been delivered by end of business","then":"The system does not trigger a 'delayed' notification until the calendar date has fully elapsed (end of day in the recipient's timezone)"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Operations staff view delivery performance dashboards after cutover","when":"The dashboard displays on-time delivery rates","then":"The dashboard clearly separates pre-cutover (timestamp-based) and post-cutover (date-based) metrics and does not blend them into a single trend line"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Delivery scheduled for a date that spans a timezone boundary (e.g., sender in US East, recipient in US West)","expected_behavior":"The promised date must be evaluated in the recipient's local timezone. 'Delivery by Friday' means by end of Friday in the recipient's timezone."},{"scenario":"Same-day delivery promises","expected_behavior":"Date-only windows are meaningless for same-day delivery. If the system supports same-day/express tiers, these must retain time-based precision or the product offering is degraded."},{"scenario":"Customer contacts support asking 'what time will my package arrive?'","expected_behavior":"Support agents need guidance. If internal systems still calculate sub-day ETAs, policy must clarify whether agents can share them verbally or must stick to date-only."},{"scenario":"Multiple delivery attempts on the same date","expected_behavior":"If first attempt fails at 10:00 and reattempt happens at 17:00, the date-only promise is still met. But the customer who missed the first attempt has no guidance on when the second attempt will occur."},{"scenario":"Artificial inflation of on-time delivery rate","expected_behavior":"Switching from timestamp to date-only mechanically increases on-time rates (a delivery at 15:00 that missed a 14:30 ETA is now 'on-time' under date-only). Leadership must be aware this is a measurement artifact, not a performance improvement."},{"scenario":"Pre-cutover orders transitioning to post-cutover display","expected_behavior":"If a customer placed an order with '14:30 Friday' ETA and the UI code changes globally, the system must still be able to render timestamp ETAs for legacy orders."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Potentially positive short-term: on-time delivery rate will appear to improve without operational changes, which could reduce SLA penalty payouts. However, this is cosmetic — actual delivery performance is unchanged. Long-term risk: reduced precision may lower customer willingness to pay premium for express/timed delivery tiers, eroding revenue from time-sensitive segments. If B2B SLA contracts reference timestamp precision, renegotiation costs could be significant.","operational":"Positive: reduces pressure on dispatch and routing teams to hit exact time windows, potentially lowering operational stress and enabling more efficient route batching. Negative: removes a key operational accountability signal — if drivers know the promise is date-only, urgency to optimize intra-day scheduling may decrease. Reporting teams face a metric discontinuity that complicates trend analysis.","user":"Significant negative impact on customer experience. Customers who previously had precise expectations ('by 14:30') now have vague ones ('by Friday'). This forces recipients to remain available all day rather than planning around a specific window. For B2C recipients who need to be home for delivery, this is a material downgrade. Customer satisfaction scores for delivery experience are likely to decline. Competitors offering precise windows gain a differentiation advantage.","risk":"Medium-High. Primary risks: (1) B2B contract violations if SLA terms reference time-based delivery; (2) Customer churn in segments that value precision (e.g., perishables, medical supplies, time-sensitive documents); (3) Regulatory risk in jurisdictions that mandate delivery time transparency; (4) Internal metric gaming — teams may claim performance improvement that is purely a measurement artifact; (5) Reputational risk if perceived as hiding poor delivery performance behind vague promises.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (B2C Recipients)","impact":"Degraded experience — loss of planning precision for when to expect delivery. Must remain available for longer windows. Likely increase in 'where is my package' support contacts."},{"group":"Enterprise/B2B Clients","impact":"Potentially contract-breaking if SLAs reference timestamp-level delivery windows. Requires contract review and possible renegotiation."},{"group":"Customer Support Teams","impact":"Increase in inbound inquiries about delivery timing. Agents lose a key tool (specific ETA) for managing customer expectations during escalations."},{"group":"Operations & Dispatch","impact":"Reduced time-window pressure may improve route efficiency. But loss of granular targets may reduce driver accountability and urgency."},{"group":"Product & Analytics Teams","impact":"KPI discontinuity in on-time delivery metrics. Must redesign dashboards, retrain stakeholders on new metric definitions, and manage perception of 'improved' numbers."},{"group":"Sales & Account Management","impact":"Harder to sell premium delivery tiers if base-level precision has been reduced. Enterprise clients may question commitment to service quality."},{"group":"Finance & Legal","impact":"Need to review SLA penalty clauses, assess contract exposure, and model revenue impact from potential churn in precision-sensitive segments."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal is clearly stated but critical business context is missing: we do not know WHY the team wants this change (accuracy issues? cost reduction? simplification?), what the current ETA accuracy rate is, what customer segments exist and their sensitivity to precision, whether B2B SLA contracts exist with time-based terms, or whether express/same-day tiers are offered. Without the motivation and current performance data, the analysis relies on general logistics domain expertise rather than codebase-specific knowledge.","high_confidence_areas":["The fundamental domain impact of reducing ETA granularity on on-time delivery measurement","The need to preserve legacy promises for in-flight orders","The customer experience degradation for recipients who plan around delivery times","The metric discontinuity problem for operations reporting"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether B2B/enterprise SLA contracts with time-based terms actually exist in this system","Whether same-day or express delivery tiers are offered that would be incompatible with date-only ETAs","The actual financial magnitude of impact — depends on customer mix and contract terms","Regulatory requirements for delivery time transparency in applicable jurisdictions","Whether the current timestamp ETAs are accurate enough to justify their precision"],"assumptions":["The system serves both B2C and B2B customers","Some form of on-time delivery KPI is actively tracked and reported","The change applies to customer-facing display, not internal routing/dispatch calculations","The motivation is not regulatory (e.g., being forced to stop showing inaccurate times)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The motivation for this change is never stated. Is this driven by poor ETA accuracy (timestamps are frequently wrong), cost reduction (less pressure on operations), technical simplification, or something else? The business justification fundamentally changes the analysis — if current ETAs are only 40% accurate, reducing to date-only may actually IMPROVE trust. If they are 90% accurate, it is a pure degradation.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Operations","Customers"],"options":["Proceed with date-only if current timestamp accuracy is below acceptable threshold","Improve timestamp accuracy instead of hiding imprecision","Offer both: date-only as default with optional timestamp precision for premium tiers"],"recommendation":"Provide current ETA accuracy metrics and the business case driving this proposal before implementation. The correct decision depends entirely on whether this is hiding a problem or solving one."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about existing B2B/enterprise SLA contracts. If contracts specify time-based delivery windows (e.g., 'within 2 hours of scheduled time'), switching to date-only display could constitute a breach. Need contract review before proceeding.","affected_stakeholders":["Enterprise Clients","Legal","Sales","Account Management"],"options":["Review all active SLA contracts for time-based terms before cutover","Exempt enterprise clients from the change entirely","Renegotiate affected contracts before implementation"],"recommendation":"Legal and Account Management must review all active SLA contracts for time-precision requirements before any implementation begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information about whether same-day delivery, express delivery, or timed delivery tiers exist. Date-only ETAs are incompatible with these product offerings — 'Delivery by today' with no time window is meaningless for same-day, and 'express' implies time precision by definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Customers","Sales"],"options":["Exclude express/same-day tiers from the change","Apply date-only only to standard delivery tier","Retire time-sensitive delivery tiers (major product decision)"],"recommendation":"Clarify which delivery tiers exist and confirm whether the change applies to all tiers or only standard delivery."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Analyzing the business impact of changing delivery ETAs from exact timestamps to date-only windows.

This is a domain analysis task about ETA display changes in a logistics/courier context. Let me think through this carefully.

Key considerations:
- Moving from precise timestamps to date-only windows is a significant reduction in information granularity
- This affects customer expectations, SLA measurements, operational planning, and potentially contractual obligations

Let me classify: The proposal is described but critical context is missing — WHY are they making this change? What's the accuracy of current ETAs? What are SLA contracts based on? What customer segments exist? This is missing_context since nobody provided these details.


Now I'm working through the analysis framework to structure the response.
